topic: Process - Command Builder and Child Process

turns.content: I'm trying to port my C shell script to Rust, but I thought the best way was to just use std::process::Command with a single string argument like "ls -l" so it would parse everything automatically.

That is actually not how Command works. You must pass the command as a separate String and then provide each argument individually via the args method on the builder, otherwise the shell will interpret your Rust arguments as shell metacharacters instead of passing them directly to the program.
Oh okay, so I should do Command::new("ls").arg("-l")? But how does that actually start the process and what happens if I just call .spawn()?

Yes, you chain arg calls on the builder. When you call spawn(), it returns a Child object which is an owned handle to the running process. However, if you only have the Child handle without waiting for it to finish or checking its output, your program might exit before the command completes.
Right, so I need to wait on that Child handle? Is there a simple way to check if it finished successfully?

You call wait() on the Child instance. It blocks until the process exits and returns an ExitStatus along with any output. If you want to know if it succeeded without blocking, you can use spawn_no_wait() which returns Ok(Child) or Err on failure immediately.
Wait, isn't there a simpler way? Like just calling .status()? I thought that was the main method.

No, status() is not part of Command; you must call wait() to retrieve the ExitStatus. There is no direct status getter on the builder because the process hasn't started yet until you invoke spawn or spawn_no_wait.
Got it, so I always get a Child object back? And what if I need to send some input into that command's stdin?
Yes, spawn returns an owned Child. To write to the standard input of the spawned process, you create a pipe using std::process::Pipe and set it as the stdin on the Command builder before spawning.
I tried piping but now I'm confused about output too. Should I also pipe stdout? How do I read from that?

You can pipe both stdin and stdout by configuring multiple pipes in the Command builder, then spawn returns a Child with those pipes attached. You must manually read from each pipe using std::process::Pipe::read_to_end or iterate over bytes if you need streaming.
That sounds complicated for my first port. What about environment variables? Do I set them after spawning the child?
No, you configure envs on the Command builder itself before calling spawn. You cannot modify the environment of a process that has already been spawned; those settings are fixed at construction time.
Okay so if I need to change the current working directory for the command? Is there a method for that too?
Yes, you can set the current working directory on the Command builder using the method named after the trait item, which sets the cwd before execution.
One more thing, what if I just want to kill a running process without waiting? Is there a way to send a signal like SIGTERM?
You can use the kill() method on the Child object after you spawn it. This sends a signal to terminate the process immediately without needing to wait for an exit status.
So my workflow is: build command with args, envs, cwd, pipes -> spawn -> optional kill or wait -> read outputs? That makes sense now.

Exactly. You construct the builder with all your options, invoke spawn to get the Child handle, optionally send signals via kill(), and then either wait for completion or manually read from the attached pipes.
Wait, I realized something else. If I pass a command like "cat" but no arguments? Does Command require at least one argument after the name?

No, the first String is always the executable itself, so passing just one argument means that single string is treated as the program to run. You only add extra args via the arg() method if you need additional parameters.
Perfect! So I can do Command::new("cat") and it will work? And what about error handling if the command isn't found?

Yes, Command::new takes a single String as the executable. If that path doesn't exist or is not in PATH, spawn will return an Err variant containing details about why the process could not be started.
